title: Why exclude food and energy from inflation measures?
gist: Explains why the FOMC anchors on core PCE inflation: it excludes food and energy because those prices are highly volatile and, given monetary policy's long-and-variable lags of about two years, a smoother, more predictable signal is better for forecasting future prices.

themes.theme: Why core PCE is the Fed's preferred gauge

insights.claim: The FOMC's preferred inflation measure is the PCE price index because it covers a broader set of household expenses than the CPI or PPI.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: PCE, not CPI, is the index the Fed actually targets, so watch core PCE prints for policy read-through even when headline CPI dominates the news.
insights.quote: Its advantage over the consumer price index (CPI) and the producer price index (PPI), for example, is that the PCE covers a broader set of household expenses.
insights.locator: The Fed and specifically the FOMC look at many price indexes

insights.claim: Core PCE excludes food and energy because those prices can fluctuate dramatically from seasonal factors or market volatility, which makes forecasting the path of prices much harder.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Food/energy spikes are noise to the Fed, so commodity-driven inflation surprises are less likely to move policy than core moves.
insights.quote: Core PCE inflation excludes food and energy because those two types of prices can fluctuate dramatically, because of seasonal factors or the high volatility of markets.
insights.locator: For this reason, the FOMC primarily focuses on core PCE inflation

insights.claim: Monetary policy is believed to act with long and variable lags averaging a couple of years, so the FOMC targets a well-predicted price measure rather than reacting to short-term fluctuations.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Because the Fed steers prices ~2 years out, near-term inflation wobbles matter less for the policy path than the trend in core measures.
insights.quote: Policy is believed to follow long and variable lags, on average, over a couple of years.
insights.locator: Although monetary policy aims at price stability

insights.claim: Core PCE has a far better signal-to-noise ratio than PCE food and PCE energy inflation, which fluctuate widely around the more stable core series.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Strip food/energy when judging the inflation trend the Fed cares about; the headline components are mostly noise.
insights.quote: In engineering parlance, the signal-to-noise ratio is much better with core PCE inflation.
insights.locator: The FRED graph above shows three series
